PR DESCRIPTION:
## What is the purpose of the change
Closes #13413 

## Brief changelog
- Added GitHub Actions to check for code style violations.

## Checklist
- [x] Make sure there is a [GitHub_issue](https://github.com/apache/dubbo/issues) field for the change (usually before you start working on it). Trivial changes like typos do not require a GitHub issue. Your pull request should address just this issue, without pulling in other changes - one PR resolves one issue.
- [X] Each commit in the pull request should have a meaningful subject line and body.
- [X] Write a pull request description that is detailed enough to understand what the pull request does, how, and why.
- [ ] Check if is necessary to patch to Dubbo 3 if you are work on Dubbo 2.7
- [ ] Write necessary unit-test to verify your logic correction, more mock a little better when cross module dependency exist. If the new feature or significant change is committed, please remember to add sample in [dubbo samples](https://github.com/apache/dubbo-samples) project.
- [ ] Add some description to [dubbo-website](https://github.com/apache/dubbo-website) project if you are requesting to add a feature.
- [X] GitHub Actions works fine on your own branch.
- [ ] If this contribution is large, please follow the [Software Donation Guide](https://github.com/apache/dubbo/wiki/Software-donation-guide).

mr.text:
SUMMARY: This PR introduces a `check-format` job leveraging `spotless:check` to enforce code style early in the CI pipeline. The fast-fail approach is architecturally sound and aligns with modern CI best practices. However, the log parsing logic is highly brittle, and several CI configuration patterns can be simplified or hardened for better maintainability and reliability.

POINTS:
- **`.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml`** (`check-format` job, log parsing step ~lines 36-40)
  - **Fragile error extraction:** The `sed` pipeline relies on exact Maven/Spotless output formatting and hardcoded job names (`Check if code needs formatting...`). This will break if Maven's log format changes, Spotless updates its output, or job names are refactored. Replace with a more robust approach like `grep -A 3 "ERROR" mvn.log | head -n 20` or skip custom parsing entirely and rely on GitHub's built-in step logs/artifact links.
  - **Suppressed real-time logging:** `mvn --log-file mvn.log` redirects all output to a file, hiding live progress in the GitHub Actions UI. Use `mvn spotless:check 2>&1 | tee mvn.log` to preserve interactive logging while still capturing the file for artifact upload.

- **`.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml`** (`needs` declarations across multiple jobs)
  - **Redundant transitive dependencies:** Jobs like `jacoco-result-merge` already depend on `unit-test`, `samples-test-result`, and `integration-test-result`, which all transitively depend on `check-format`. GitHub Actions automatically resolves transitive dependencies, so explicitly adding `needs: check-format` to these downstream jobs is unnecessary and clutters the DAG.

- **`.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml`** (`check-format` job definition ~lines 21-48)
  - **Missing timeout:** The new job lacks a `timeout-minutes` directive. Add `timeout-minutes: 10` to prevent indefinite hangs in case of Maven/Spotless deadlocks or network issues.
  - **Action version inconsistency:** The new job uses `actions/checkout@v4` and `actions/setup-java@v4`, while existing jobs still reference `@v3`. Standardize on `@v4` across the entire workflow for consistent behavior, security patches, and easier maintenance.

- **`.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml`** (`check-format` job, failure step ~lines 43-48)
  - **Overly complex failure step:** Using `actions/github-script` with `core.setFailed()` works, but since `continue-on-error: true` is already set on the check step, a simpler `run: exit 1` in a standard shell step achieves the same result with less overhead and no additional action dependency.

mr_code.text:
SUMMARY:
The PR introduces a `check-format` job to enforce code style using `spotless:check` before other CI jobs run. This is a solid addition for maintaining code quality. However, there are a few areas regarding tool consistency, log parsing robustness, and workflow dependency optimization that should be addressed before merging.

POINTS:
- **`.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml` (Line 33)**: Use the Maven Wrapper (`./mvnw`) instead of `mvn`. The rest of the workflow relies on `./mvnw` to guarantee a consistent Maven version and avoid potential `command not found` errors on fresh runners. Change `mvn --log-file mvn.log spotless:check` to `./mvnw --log-file mvn.log spotless:check`.
- **`.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml` (Lines 44-46)**: The `sed` command for generating the PR summary is fragile and likely ineffective with `--log-file`. When Maven redirects output to a file, it does not include the GitHub Actions console prefixes, so the regex targeting job/step names will not match. Additionally, `head -n -11` is a GNU-specific extension that arbitrarily drops the last 11 lines. Simplify this to `grep "\[ERROR\]" mvn.log | head -n 20 >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` to reliably surface the first 20 errors, and rely on the uploaded artifact for full diagnostics.
- **`.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml` (Lines 87, 193, 277, 344, 381, 448, 468)**: Redundant `needs: check-format` declarations. GitHub Actions automatically resolves transitive dependencies. Since `unit-test`, `samples-test-job`, `integration-test-job`, etc., already depend on jobs that now require `check-format`, explicitly adding `check-format` to their `needs` arrays is unnecessary. You can safely remove `check-format` from the `needs` lists of downstream jobs, keeping it only on the top-level jobs (`license`, `build-source`, `unit-test-prepare`, `samples-test-prepare`, `integration-test-prepare`, `error-code-inspecting`, `native-image-inspecting`) to keep the YAML cleaner.
- **`.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml` (Line 30)**: Action version consistency. The new job uses `@v4` for `checkout`, `setup-java`, and `upload-artifact`, while the existing workflow uses `@v3`. While using newer versions is acceptable, consider either updating the rest of the workflow to `@v4` for consistency, or pinning to `@v3` here to match the existing baseline.
- **General**: Ensure the `spotless-maven-plugin` is properly configured in the root `pom.xml` (or in a companion PR) before this workflow is merged, otherwise the `spotless:check` goal will fail with a plugin resolution error.

mr_code_tools.text:
SUMMARY:
PR adds a new `check-format` GitHub Actions job running `mvn spotless:check` to enforce code style before other CI jobs. While the feature aligns with the project's existing spotless Maven plugin configuration (v2.42.0 with palantirJavaFormat), there are issues around action version inconsistency, fragile log parsing, unnecessary complexity in failure handling, and significant CI pipeline serialization impact.

POINTS:
- [.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml:24-27] **Action version inconsistency**: The new `check-format` job uses `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-java@v4`, and `actions/upload-artifact@v4`, while all existing jobs in the workflow consistently use `@v3` (confirmed across license, build-source, unit-test, samples-test, integration-test, jacoco-result-merge, error-code-inspecting, and native-image-inspecting jobs). For consistency, use `@v3` versions to match the repo's established pattern.

- [.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml:45] **Fragile log parsing in summary generation**: The sed pipeline `sed 's/Check if code needs formatting    Check if code aligns with code style   [0-9A-Z:.-]\+//'` hardcodes the job/step names to strip Maven log prefixes. If step names are renamed in the future, this silently breaks. Consider using a simpler grep pattern that doesn't depend on specific step names, e.g. `grep "ERROR" | grep -v "\[ERROR\] BUILD"` to extract formatting issues.

- [.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml:26-61] **Redundant failure mechanism**: Using `continue-on-error: true` on the spotless check step, then manually calling `core.setFailed()` in a subsequent `actions/github-script` step, adds unnecessary complexity. The `spotless:check` command returns a non-zero exit code on failure, which naturally fails the job. If artifact upload on failure is the reason for `continue-on-error`, use `if: always()` on the upload step instead, and let spotless fail the job naturally without the extra script step.

- [.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml:69-568] **Pipeline serialization impact**: Adding `needs: check-format` to ALL downstream jobs (license, build-source, unit-test-prepare, samples-test-prepare, integration-test-prepare, error-code-inspecting, native-image-inspecting, and transitively to result aggregation jobs) serializes the entire CI pipeline. Previously, these jobs could start in parallel immediately. Now, developers must wait for the spotless check (~1-2 min) before seeing any test results, adding latency to every PR. Consider whether `check-format` should gate only final aggregation steps (jacoco-result-merge, etc.) rather than all jobs, allowing tests to run in parallel while spotless runs.

- [.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml:26-31] **Maven cache not configured for spotless job**: The `check-format` job runs `mvn spotless:check` without Maven repository caching, while other jobs use `actions/cache@v3` for `~/.m2/repository`. The spotless plugin depends on `com.alibaba:dubbo-shared-resources:1.0.0` (which provides `dubbo-importorder.txt` — confirmed this file does not exist locally in the repo). Without cache, this dependency downloads on every run. Add Maven caching to improve reliability and speed.

- [.github/workflows/build-and-test-pr.yml:33-37] **Overlap with existing checkstyle validation**: The existing `license` job (apache/skywalking-eyes) and `build-source` job's checkstyle (checkstyle v8.45.1) already validate license headers and unused imports (checkstyle.xml includes `UnusedImports`, `RedundantImport`). Spotless performs overlapping checks on the same issues. The fast-fail benefit is valuable, but developers may see duplicate error reports for license headers and unused imports from both spotless and checkstyle in CI summaries.

SUMMARY:
The PR updates the ESLint client configuration to dynamically resolve the project root (highest directory containing `package.json`), set `workingDirectory` to `auto`, and adjust `nodePath` and `codeAction` settings to better align with the ESLint VSCode extension. The approach is conceptually sound but introduces several robustness, safety, and performance concerns that should be addressed before merging.

POINTS:
- `ESLintClientImpl.java` (L~38-40): `configurationItem.getScopeUri()` can return `null`. Passing `null` to `new URI()` throws a `NullPointerException`. Add a null check before URI construction. Additionally, `new File(URI)` only supports `file:` scheme URIs; non-file URIs will throw `IllegalArgumentException`. Consider using `URI.create()` or gracefully fallback to a workspace root if the URI is invalid or non-file.
- `ESLintClientImpl.java` (L~40): Typo: double semicolon `;;` after `getParentFile()`.
- `ESLintClientImpl.java` (L~49, 55): If no `package.json` is found during traversal, `highestPackageJsonDir` remains `null`. Subsequent calls to `.toURI()` and `.getAbsolutePath()` will throw `NullPointerException`. Provide a safe fallback (e.g., workspace root or current working directory) when the search yields no results.
- `ESLintClientImpl.java` (L~46-48): Swallowing `URISyntaxException` without logging or fallback is unsafe. At minimum, log a warning via `Platform.getLog()` or a standard logger, and ensure `highestPackageJsonDir` is initialized to a valid fallback before the `try` block.
- `ESLintClientImpl.java` (L~31-61): The `configuration()` method is invoked frequently by the LSP client. Synchronously walking the file system on every call can cause noticeable UI lag, especially in large monorepos. Consider caching the resolved root per workspace/document, or leveraging Eclipse's `IWorkspaceRoot`/`IProject` APIs if available in this context.
- `ESLintClientImpl.java` (L~59-60): `Map.of()` requires Java 9+. Verify the plugin's minimum Java version. If Java 8 compatibility is still required, replace with `Collections.singletonMap` or `new HashMap<>()`.
- `ESLintClientImpl.java` (L~55): The comment states `nodePath` should point to `node_modules`. According to the ESLint VSCode extension documentation, `nodePath` expects the path to the `node` executable, not a directory containing `node_modules`. Passing a directory may cause the extension to fail to locate the runtime. Verify this against the extension's configuration schema; you may need to append `/node` or use a different setting if the intent is to resolve modules.

mr_code.text:
SUMMARY:
The PR improves the ESLint VSCode integration by dynamically resolving the workspace root (highest directory containing `package.json`), configuring `workingDirectory` to `auto` mode, and correctly setting `nodePath` to point to the project root instead of the Node.js executable. The core logic aligns well with the PR description and ESLint server expectations. However, the implementation lacks robust null/error handling, contains a minor typo, and could crash in edge cases where URI parsing fails or configuration items are missing.

POINTS:
- **Line 33**: `configurationParams.getItems().get(0)` assumes the list is non-empty. If the server sends an empty list, this will throw `IndexOutOfBoundsException`. Add a size check: `if (configurationParams.getItems().isEmpty()) return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(Collections.emptyList());`
- **Line 33/42**: `configurationItem.getScopeUri()` can be `null`. Passing `null` to `new URI(...)` will throw `NullPointerException`. Add a null guard before URI parsing.
- **Line 42**: Double semicolon typo: `getParentFile();;`. Remove the extra semicolon.
- **Line 42-51**: If `URISyntaxException` is caught, or if `getParentFile()` returns `null`, `highestPackageJsonDir` remains `null`. This will cause a `NullPointerException` on line 51 when calling `.toURI()`. Consider falling back to a default workspace directory (e.g., `new File(System.getProperty("user.dir"))`) or returning a minimal config map to prevent server crashes.
- **Line 48-50**: The `catch (URISyntaxException e)` block silently ignores the exception. Even if rare, logging it (e.g., via `Platform.getLog(ESLintClientImpl.class).logError(...)`) is strongly recommended for debugging integration issues.
- **Line 51**: `highestPackageJsonDir.toURI().toString()` works, but `highestPackageJsonDir.toPath().toUri().toString()` is more consistent with modern `java.nio.file` practices and handles path normalization better.
- **Line 58**: `Map.of()` requires Java 9+. While modern Eclipse plugins typically target Java 11+, verify the project's minimum Java version. If compatibility with older runtimes is needed, replace with `Collections.singletonMap` or a mutable `HashMap`.
- **General**: The directory traversal logic correctly finds the root `package.json`, but note that `new File(uri).getParentFile()` may behave unexpectedly if `scopeUri` points to a directory rather than a file. Consider normalizing the URI to a `Path` first: `Path path = Paths.get(new URI(configurationItem.getScopeUri()));` and traversing via `path.getParent()`.

This PR fixes ESLint VSCode integration by correctly setting nodePath, workspaceFolder, and workingDirectory configuration properties. The changes introduce several null-safety issues and edge case bugs that need addressing before merge.

- [ESLintClientImpl.java:42] `configurationItem.getScopeUri()` could be null. `new URI(null)` throws `NullPointerException`. Add a null check: `if (configurationItem.getScopeUri() == null) { ... }` before constructing the URI.

- [ESLintClientImpl.java:50-51] Null safety gap: If `URISyntaxException` is caught (line 48-49), `highestPackageJsonDir` remains `null`. The subsequent `highestPackageJsonDir.toURI().toString()` on line 50 and `highestPackageJsonDir.getAbsolutePath()` on line 55 will throw NPE. The silent catch block swallows the exception leaving invalid state. Either log the error, return a fallback config, or rethrow as a runtime exception.

- [ESLintClientImpl.java:50] Logic bug when no `package.json` exists: If no package.json is found while walking up, `highestPackageJsonDir` retains the initial value (the file's parent directory via `getParentFile()`). This means `nodePath` and `workspaceFolder` point to an arbitrary directory that may not contain node_modules or be a valid workspace root. Consider falling back to the original `NodeJSManager.getNodeJsLocation()` behavior or returning a minimal config when no package.json is found.

- [ESLintClientImpl.java:33] Potential `IndexOutOfBoundsException` / `NullPointerException`: `configurationParams.getItems().get(0)` has no guard against an empty list or null. The subagent confirmed this is a new pattern in this codebase with no existing precedent for defensive coding. Add: `if (configurationParams.getItems() == null || configurationParams.getItems().isEmpty()) { return ...fallback... }`

- [ESLintClientImpl.java:42] `new File(new URI(...)).getParentFile()` can return `null` for root URIs (e.g., `file:///`). When `parentFile` is null, the while loop is skipped and `highestPackageJsonDir` is null, causing downstream NPEs on lines 50 and 55. Add null guard.

- [ESLintClientImpl.java:57] Minor: Consider extracting the nested `Map.of("disableRuleComment", Map.of(...))` into a named constant or helper method for readability, though this is cosmetic.

SUMMARY: This PR removes informational console messages from `ScanUtil.scanFluo()` and `ScanUtil.scanAccumulo()`, drops empty-result tracking, and changes error handling to print to `System.err` followed by `System.exit(-1)`. While reducing console noise may align with issue #981, introducing `System.exit()` in a utility method is a significant anti-pattern that breaks caller control, hinders testability, and bypasses proper exception propagation. Additionally, relying on `System.out`/`System.err` instead of a logging framework is inconsistent with modern Java library standards.

POINTS:
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (Lines ~130-131, ~170-171): **Avoid `System.exit(-1)` in utility methods.** Calling `System.exit()` abruptly terminates the JVM, skipping `finally` blocks, preventing callers from catching/handling exceptions, and making unit testing nearly impossible. Replace with `throw new RuntimeException("Scan failed", e);` or allow the existing `FluoException`/`Exception` to propagate naturally.
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (Lines ~125-127): **Removal of "No data found" feedback.** Stripping the empty-result message may leave CLI users uncertain whether the scan succeeded or simply returned zero matches. If this is a user-facing utility, consider retaining a minimal message, adding a `--quiet`/`--verbose` flag, or routing it to `LOG.info()` instead of hard-removing it.
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (Lines ~93-94, ~150-151): **Removal of scan initiation messages.** Removing `"Scanning snapshot..."` and `"Scanning data in Accumulo..."` eliminates user feedback. If `ScanUtil` is intended as a library component, these `System.out` calls should have been migrated to a logger long ago. If it's a CLI helper, preserve them behind a logging level or CLI flag to maintain usability.
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (Lines ~130, ~170): **Prefer a logging framework over `System.err`.** While switching errors to `System.err` is an improvement over `System.out`, production Java code should use SLF4J or java.util.logging. `System.err` bypasses log aggregation, structured formatting, and level-based filtering, making debugging and monitoring harder in distributed environments.
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (PR Title/Context): **Clarify PR title intent.** The title `"fixes #981 stopped printing meta info in scan"` reads ambiguously. If the goal was to *stop* printing meta info, rephrase to `"stops printing meta info in scan"` or `"removes verbose scan output"`. Clear titles improve changelog readability and reduce reviewer confusion.

SUMMARY: This PR removes informational/status messages from `stdout` to prevent interference with downstream parsing or piping, moves error messages to `stderr`, and ensures a non-zero exit code on failure. These are standard and appropriate improvements for a CLI utility intended for scripting. The license header whitespace cleanup is also a minor positive.

- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (L94-95, L143-144): Removing the `"Scanning snapshot/data..."` messages is correct for tools meant to be piped. If interactive users later request progress feedback, consider adding a `--verbose` flag rather than re-adding unconditionally.
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (L133-135): Removing the `"No data found"` message aligns with Unix CLI conventions (silent success on empty output). Ensure this matches the expected behavior for automation scripts. If needed, users can check `stdout` length or add a wrapper to detect empty results.
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (L138-139, L179-180): Switching to `System.err` and `System.exit(-1)` is appropriate for CLI error handling. Note that `ScanUtil` is a utility class; calling `System.exit()` directly can be risky if this code is ever invoked programmatically outside of a CLI entry point. If this is strictly a CLI command handler, it's fine. Otherwise, consider throwing a custom unchecked exception or using a logger.
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (L138, L179): Printing only `e.getMessage()` to `stderr` may hide stack traces needed for debugging complex failures. For a production CLI, consider using a logging framework (e.g., SLF4J) or at least `e.printStackTrace()` in debug/verbose modes. The current approach is acceptable for simple user-facing errors.
- `modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java` (L7, L9): Trailing whitespace removal in the license header is a minor but appreciated cleanup.

SUMMARY: This PR refactors the Tycho pomless extension to support arbitrary aggregator directories by removing hardcoded name checks and introducing a strict mapping priority order. It also updates the `polyglot-common` dependency to a SNAPSHOT version. While the architectural shift towards priority-based mapping aligns with the stated goals, the implementation of the aggregator scanning logic contains a critical state-management flaw that will break multi-directory or parallel builds.

POINTS:
- **`tycho-extras/tycho-pomless/pom.xml`**: Dependency on `0.4.9-SNAPSHOT` should be replaced with a stable release before merging. SNAPSHOT dependencies in build infrastructure compromise reproducible builds and can introduce unexpected upstream changes during CI/CD pipelines.
- **`tycho-extras/tycho-pomless/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/pomless/AbstractTychoMapping.java`**: Removing `getPriority()` from the abstract class is correct given the new priority scheme, but verify that all subclasses (`TychoBundleMapping`, `TychoFeatureMapping`, `TychoRepositoryMapping`, etc.) now explicitly override it. Missing overrides will cause compilation failures or fallback to unexpected defaults.
- **`tycho-extras/tycho-pomless/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/pomless/TychoAggregatorMapping.java`**:
  - **Critical:** `ThreadLocal<Boolean> IS_FIRST` fundamentally breaks the intended behavior. It ensures the module scan runs exactly once per thread, meaning only the first aggregator directory processed on a given thread will be recognized. Maven/Polyglot often processes directories across multiple threads or sequentially on the same thread. This will cause subsequent aggregator directories to be silently skipped. Replace with an instance-level boolean or directory-specific tracking to allow scanning of all valid aggregator directories.
  - **Resource Management:** Using `file.deleteOnExit()` for the generated `pom.tycho` is an anti-pattern in build tools. It defers cleanup until JVM termination, which can cause disk bloat, file-lock conflicts, or stale artifacts in CI/long-running environments. Consider using `Files.createTempFile()` with explicit cleanup, or leverage Polyglot's model lifecycle for temporary artifacts.
  - **Path Validation:** `Path.of(location).endsWith(TYCHO_POM)` is functional but `Path.of(location).getFileName().toString().equals(TYCHO_POM)` is safer and more explicit against path separator edge cases or relative path quirks.
  - **Diff Completeness:** The `} else {` block at the end of `getPrimaryArtifact()` appears empty in the provided diff. Ensure no fallback logic, validation, or logging was accidentally stripped during refactoring.

SUMMARY:
This PR removes the hardcoded `tycho.pomless.aggregator.names` property and leverages polyglot-maven's priority-based mapping resolution to automatically detect aggregator directories. It updates the polyglot dependency to a SNAPSHOT version that optimizes `locatePom()` calls by trying mappings in priority order. While the architectural direction is correct, the implementation introduces a fragile `ThreadLocal` mechanism to control directory scanning, which directly conflicts with the new priority-based resolution order and will likely cause aggregator detection to fail silently. Additionally, there's a mismatch between the described behavior (scanning root `pom.xml` modules) and the actual code (`listFiles()`).

POINTS:
- `tycho-extras/tycho-pomless/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/pomless/TychoAggregatorMapping.java` (L60-85): The `ThreadLocal<Boolean> IS_FIRST` logic is fundamentally flawed when combined with polyglot-maven's priority-based resolution. Since `TychoAggregatorMapping` has a priority of `-10f`, it will be evaluated *after* higher-priority mappings (Bundle, Feature, Repository, Target). By the time `getPrimaryArtifact()` is invoked for an aggregator directory, `IS_FIRST` will already be `false` (flipped by the first higher-priority mapping call on any directory), causing the method to return `null` and skip aggregator detection entirely. Consider using a `Set<File>` to track already-scanned directories, or check if the current `dir` matches the multi-module project root instead of relying on call order.
- `tycho-extras/tycho-pomless/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/pomless/TychoAggregatorMapping.java` (L78): `IS_FIRST.remove()` is nested inside the `if (subFolders != null)` block. If `dir.listFiles()` returns `null`, the `ThreadLocal` state is never cleaned up, leading to state leakage across subsequent builds in the same JVM/thread. State cleanup should be guaranteed (e.g., via a `finally` block or by removing the `ThreadLocal` entirely in favor of a safer tracking mechanism).
- `tycho-extras/tycho-pomless/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/pomless/TychoAggregatorMapping.java` (L63-75): The PR description states the change "simply scans all modules listed (transitively) in the root pom.xml for directly nested modules", but the implementation uses `dir.listFiles()` to scan immediate subdirectories. This doesn't match the description and may incorrectly include non-module directories or miss expected modules. Clarify if the intent is to parse the parent `pom.xml`'s `<modules>` section or if physical directory scanning is the intended behavior.
- `tycho-extras/tycho-pomless/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/pomless/AbstractTychoMapping.java` (L235): Removing `getPriority()` from the abstract class is acceptable if subclasses implement it or rely on the interface default. However, the PR description emphasizes a "distinct order of Tycho-Mappings". Ensure that `TychoBundleMapping`, `TychoFeatureMapping`, `TychoRepositoryMapping`, and `TychoTargetMapping` explicitly define their priorities to guarantee the intended resolution order across different polyglot-maven versions and avoid relying on implicit defaults.
- `tycho-extras/tycho-pomless/pom.xml` (L50): The dependency on `polyglot-common:0.4.9-SNAPSHOT` is noted as required for the priority optimization. Please ensure this SNAPSHOT is published to a reachable repository (e.g., Sonatype OSSRH or Eclipse Nexus) before merging, or coordinate with the polyglot-maven team to release a stable version first to avoid build failures for downstream consumers.
- `tycho-its/projects/pomless-model/...` (test data additions): Adding `target/some.data` and `otherFolder/nested/aFile.txt` is a good practice to verify that the aggregator correctly ignores build artifacts and deeply nested non-module directories. SUMMARY: This PR replaces an unbounded `newCachedThreadPool()` in `LocalDataTransferServer` with a configurable, bounded `ThreadPoolExecutor` backed by a `SynchronousQueue`. It introduces a new configuration property (`rubix.local.transfer.max-threads`) with a default of `4096`, and adds a utility method for consistent thread naming. The changes improve resource governance and observability, which is a solid step for production stability.

POINTS:
- `rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java` (L179-184): **Thread Pool Lifecycle & Rejection Policy** – The `ExecutorService` is instantiated locally inside `run()` and never explicitly shut down. While `corePoolSize=0` and `keepAliveTime=60s` will eventually reclaim idle threads, explicit shutdown (e.g., via `try-finally` or promoting to an instance field) is safer for clean server lifecycle management. Additionally, `SynchronousQueue` combined with a bounded `maxPoolSize` will trigger the default `AbortPolicy` (`RejectedExecutionException`) when all threads are saturated. Consider adding a `RejectedExecutionHandler` (e.g., `CallerRunsPolicy` or a custom handler that logs/drops gracefully) to prevent hard failures under peak load.
- `rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CacheConfig.java` (L161): **Default Value Behavioral Change** – Changing `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` from `Integer.MAX_VALUE` to `4096` is a breaking behavioral change for deployments that relied on unbounded scaling. Ensure this is documented in release notes/configuration guides. Also, evaluate if `4096` is optimal across all target environments; a lower default (e.g., `1024` or CPU-core-based) may be safer to prevent context-switching overhead on resource-constrained nodes.
- `rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CacheConfig.java` (L333-336): **Constant Naming Clarity** – `getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads()` falls back to `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS`. While functionally correct, reusing a generic constant for a specific component can cause maintenance confusion. Consider renaming the constant to `DEFAULT_LOCAL_TRANSFER_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` or adding a clarifying comment to indicate intentional reuse.
- `rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CommonUtilities.java` (L88-95): **Daemon Thread Consideration** – `ThreadFactoryBuilder().build()` creates non-daemon threads by default. For background worker pools in a long-running server, daemon threads are often preferred to allow the JVM to exit cleanly when the main process terminates. Consider chaining `.setDaemon(true)` unless non-daemon behavior is explicitly required for graceful shutdown hooks.
- **General**: The use of `SynchronousQueue` correctly mimics `newCachedThreadPool()` semantics while enforcing the upper bound. The addition of named threads (`lds-worker-%s`) will significantly improve stack trace readability and metrics attribution. Overall, a well-scoped improvement with minor lifecycle/policy considerations noted above.

SUMMARY:
This PR replaces an unbounded `Executors.newCachedThreadPool()` with a configurable, bounded `ThreadPoolExecutor` for the `LocalDataTransferServer`. It introduces a new configuration property for max threads, sets a sensible default (`4096`), and adds a utility for named threads to improve observability. The changes are well-structured and address a valid stability concern. However, there is a critical runtime behavior change regarding task rejection that needs attention before merging.

POINTS:
- **`rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java`**
  - **Critical: Missing Rejection Handling** (Lines 179-183): Switching to a bounded `ThreadPoolExecutor` with a `SynchronousQueue` means that when `maxPoolSize` is reached, `execute()` will throw a `RejectedExecutionException` (default `AbortPolicy`). Since this call is inside a `while(true)` loop without a `try-catch`, the server thread will crash and stop accepting new connections under load. 
    *Recommendation*: Either wrap `threadPool.execute(cliThread)` in a `try-catch` for `RejectedExecutionException` to log and continue, or pass `new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy()` as the 6th argument. `CallerRunsPolicy` is often preferred for network servers as it naturally back-pressures the `accept()` loop when the pool is saturated.
  - **Minor: Executor Lifecycle**: The `threadPool` is never shut down in the `stop()` method or on exception. While this is a pre-existing issue, consider adding `threadPool.shutdownNow()` in the `stop()` method or a `finally` block to prevent thread leaks during server restarts.

- **`rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CacheConfig.java`**
  - **Default Value Change** (Line 161): Changing `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` from `Integer.MAX_VALUE` to `4096` is a good safety measure. Since this constant is also used by `getServerMaxThreads()` (likely for `BookKeeperServer` per the PR title), ensure `4096` is an appropriate default for both servers. If they have different workload profiles, consider introducing a separate `DEFAULT_LOCAL_TRANSFER_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` constant to avoid coupling their defaults.
  - **Config Key Naming** (Line 72): `rubix.local.transfer.max-threads` follows existing naming conventions and is clear. No changes needed.

- **`rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CommonUtilities.java`**
  - **Thread Naming Utility** (Lines 88-95): Using `ThreadFactoryBuilder` is a clean approach for observability. The format string `"lds-worker-%s"` in the caller is descriptive. Consider making the format string configurable or adding a default prefix like `"rubix-lds-worker-%s"` to avoid naming collisions if multiple Rubix components run in the same JVM.
  - **Imports**: Added `com.google.common.util.concurrent.ThreadFactoryBuilder`. Guava is already a dependency in this module, so this is safe.

- **General**
  - The diff cleanly separates configuration, utility, and server logic. The use of `SynchronousQueue` with `corePoolSize=0` correctly mimics cached thread pool semantics while enforcing the upper bound. Once the rejection policy is addressed, this is a solid improvement for production stability.

SUMMARY:
PR bounds the LocalDataTransferServer thread pool (was unbounded `newCachedThreadPool`) and changes `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` from `Integer.MAX_VALUE` to `4096`, which also bounds the already-configured BookKeeperServer. Good motivation, but the thread pool lifecycle is broken and the rejection behavior under load needs attention.

POINTS:
- [rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java:182] **Thread pool is a local variable — cannot be shutdown.** The `ExecutorService` is created inside `run()` but `stop()` (line ~213) only closes `listener`. The thread pool should be a class-level field so `stop()` can call `threadPool.shutdown()` and `threadPool.awaitTermination()`. Currently, on server stop, in-flight client tasks are abandoned and the thread pool is orphaned until GC.

- [rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java:186] **SynchronousQueue will reject tasks when pool is full — no rejection policy specified.** With `corePoolSize=0`, `maxPoolSize=4096`, and `SynchronousQueue`, once 4096 threads are busy, new tasks will be rejected with `RejectedExecutionException` (default `AbortPolicy`). Under high concurrent load this will cause client connections to fail silently (the exception is swallowed by the `catch (IOException e)` block). Consider using a bounded `LinkedBlockingQueue` instead (e.g., `new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(1000)`) so excess tasks queue rather than fail, or add an explicit `RejectedExecutionHandler`.

- [rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CommonUtilities.java:92] **ThreadFactory does not set daemon threads — inconsistent with rest of codebase.** Other ThreadFactory implementations in the codebase (`Hadoop2ClusterManager.java:57`, `CachingInputStream.java:68`) explicitly call `t.setDaemon(true)`. The new `threadsNamed()` utility should support setting daemon status, or `LocalDataTransferServer` should ensure its worker threads are daemon threads to avoid blocking JVM shutdown.

- [rubix-spi/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/spi/CacheConfig.java:72] **Config key naming is inconsistent — may confuse users.** New key `KEY_LOCAL_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` is `"rubix.local.transfer.max-threads"`, but the default value `DEFAULT_SERVER_MAX_THREADS` is shared with the existing `getServerMaxThreads()` whose key is `"rubix.cache.bookkeeper.max-threads"` (line ~125). Users may not realize that `rubix.local.transfer.max-threads` and `rubix.cache.bookkeeper.max-threads` both default to the same value but are independent configs. Consider documenting this relationship or using a shared default constant name that reflects its broader scope.

- [rubix-bookkeeper/src/main/java/com/qubole/rubix/bookkeeper/LocalDataTransferServer.java:180] **Minor: `getServerMaxThreads` is imported but never used in this file.** The import at line 49 is unused since the PR uses `getLocalTransferServerMaxThreads` instead. Clean up the unnecessary import.

SUMMARY: The PR correctly implements support for bounded wildcard types (`? extends` and `? super`) in generic type arguments by mapping them to Moshi's `Types.subtypeOf()` and `Types.supertypeOf()` helper methods. The implementation safely handles JavaPoet's internal representation of wildcards and fails fast for unsupported multi-bound wildcards. Tests are appropriately updated to verify the generated adapter code.

POINTS:
- `auto-value-moshi/src/main/java/com/ryanharter/auto/value/moshi/AutoValueMoshiExtension.java` (L493-500): Checking `lowerBounds.size() == 1` before `upperBounds` is crucial. JavaPoet represents `? super T` with `lowerBounds = [T]` and `upperBounds = [Object.class]`. Prioritizing lower bounds ensures `? super String` correctly generates `Types.supertypeOf(String.class)` instead of incorrectly falling back to `subtypeOf(Object.class)`.
- `auto-value-moshi/src/main/java/com/ryanharter/auto/value/moshi/AutoValueMoshiExtension.java` (L501-503): Throwing `IllegalArgumentException` for multiple bounds is appropriate. Moshi's `Types` API cannot represent intersection types (e.g., `? extends Foo & Bar`), so failing at annotation processing time with a clear message prevents confusing downstream compilation errors.
- `auto-value-moshi/src/main/java/com/ryanharter/auto/value/moshi/AutoValueMoshiExtension.java` (L505): Using `$T.class` for the bound type works correctly for standard class/interface bounds. Note that if a wildcard bounds a parameterized type (e.g., `? extends Map<String, String>`), `$T.class` will generate `Map.class`, which aligns with Moshi's `subtypeOf(Class<?>)` signature that only accepts raw classes anyway.
- `auto-value-moshi/src/test/java/com/ryanharter/auto/value/moshi/AutoValueMoshiExtensionTest.java` (L75, L78, L127, L130): The test updates correctly reflect the new behavior. Changing `Set<String>` to `Set<? extends String>` and `Map<String, Set<String>>` to `Map<String, Set<? super String>>` effectively validates both `subtypeOf` and `supertypeOf` code paths.
- Minor suggestion: Consider adding a quick test case for an unbounded wildcard `?` (e.g., `List<?>`) to explicitly verify it compiles and generates `Types.subtypeOf(Object.class)`, ensuring the `upperBounds.size() == 1` fallback behaves as expected. (Optional but good for completeness)

All changes are clean, well-targeted, and resolve the reported issue without introducing regressions. LGTM.

SUMMARY:
PR adds `ForbidCertainMethodCheck` to ban specified methods by name regex and/or argument count. While the check concept is useful (Issue #604), there are critical bugs: a missing message key causing runtime `MissingResourceException`, and incorrect token registration (`EXPR` instead of `METHOD_CALL`) that misses method calls in common contexts like method arguments, ternary expressions, and annotations.

POINTS:
- [sevntu-checks/src/main/resources/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/messages.properties] **Missing message key**: The violation message key `forbid.certain.method` is referenced in `checkstyle-metadata.xml` (`<message-key key="forbid.certain.method"/>`) and in `ForbidCertainMethodCheck.java` but is NOT defined in `messages.properties`. This will cause a `MissingResourceException` at runtime when a violation is logged. Add: `forbid.certain.method={0} method with {1} argument(s) is forbidden.`

- [sevntu-checks/src/main/java/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/ForbidCertainMethodCheck.java] **Incorrect token registration**: The check uses `TokenTypes.EXPR` in `getDefaultTokens()` and then searches for `METHOD_CALL` within the EXPR node via `Utils.getNextSubTreeNode()`. This **misses method calls in non-statement contexts**: as method arguments (`foo(bar())`), in annotations (`@Ann(method())`), in ternary expressions (`cond ? method() : other()`), in lambdas (`x -> method()`), and in variable initializers. The correct approach is to register `TokenTypes.METHOD_CALL` directly, as demonstrated by `CheckstyleTestMakeupCheck.java:128`, `OverridableMethodInConstructorCheck.java:420`, and `EitherLogOrThrowCheck.java:340`. No other check in the project uses `EXPR` for method call detection.

- [sevntu-checks/src/main/java/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/ForbidCertainMethodCheck.java] **Validate argument count range for logical errors**: The range parser should validate that min ≤ max in bounded ranges (e.g., reject "5-2"). While the format validation may parse this, the semantic validation should reject inverted ranges during configuration with a clear error message.

- [sevntu-checks/src/main/java/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/ForbidCertainMethodCheck.java] **Constructor detection uses same "method" message**: When forbidding constructors via `LITERAL_NEW`, the violation message uses `forbid.certain.method` which says "method" not "constructor". Consider either adding a separate message key for constructor violations or updating the message to say "method/constructor" for clarity.

- [eclipsecs-sevntu-plugin/src/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/checkstyle-metadata.xml:182] **Default `argumentCount="0-"` may be unexpected**: The default value `0-` means "0 or more arguments", which effectively matches ALL argument counts. Combined with `methodName="^$"` (matches nothing), the default is "forbid nothing". This is reasonable for safety, but the description should clarify that the default configuration forbids nothing and requires explicit `methodName` to be effective.

- [sevntu-checks/src/test/java/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/ForbidCertainMethodCheckTest.java] **Verify test coverage for nested method calls**: Ensure tests include method calls in non-statement contexts (as arguments, in ternary expressions, in annotations). Without these, the current `EXPR`-based approach's limitations would go undetected.

- [sevntu-checks/src/main/java/com/github/sevntu/checkstyle/checks/coding/ForbidCertainMethodCheck.java] **Add null check for parsed regex**: The `setMethodName` method compiles the regex pattern. Verify that when `methodName` is `null`, the code handles it gracefully (treats as "match nothing" or logs a configuration warning) rather than throwing a `NullPointerException`.

SUMMARY: This PR successfully upgrades the project from JDK 21 to JDK 23 across Maven modules, CI/CD pipelines, and IDE configurations. The architectural split between CLI (standard GraalVM) and GUI (Gluon GraalVM) workflows is logically sound for JavaFX/native-image builds. However, there are several CI stability risks, deprecated Maven properties, and JDK lifecycle considerations that should be addressed before merging.

POINTS:
- **`.github/workflows/build.yml` & `.github/workflows/docker-publish.yml`**: Using `gluonhq/setup-graalvm@master` introduces CI instability and non-reproducible builds. Pin to a specific release tag (e.g., `@v1` or a semantic version) to ensure consistent runner behavior across PRs and prevent breakage from upstream action updates.
- **`CLI/pom.xml`, `Core/pom.xml`, `GUI/pom.xml`**: `maven.compiler.source` and `maven.compiler.target` are deprecated in modern Maven Compiler Plugin versions. Replace them with `<maven.compiler.release>23</maven.compiler.release>` to enforce strict API compatibility, simplify cross-compilation, and align with current Maven best practices.
- **`.github/workflows/build.yml`**: The architecture fallback `${{ matrix.os == 'macos-14' && 'aarch64' || 'x86_64' }}` is fragile and may misconfigure if GitHub updates runner labels or adds new macOS versions. Consider using `${{ runner.arch }}` or explicitly defining `arch` in the matrix strategy for clarity and maintainability.
- **`GUI/pom.xml`**: The `gluonfx-maven-plugin.version` property is correctly added, but verify that the plugin configuration references `${gluonfx-maven-plugin.version}` instead of a hardcoded value. Additionally, ensure the updated `reachability-metadata.json` and `resource-config.json` (listed as changed but truncated in the diff) are validated against GraalVM 23's native-image scanner, as JDK 23 introduces internal API changes that frequently require reflection/resource config adjustments.
- **`.idea/misc.xml`**: Committing IDE configuration is acceptable for team standardization, but ensure it doesn't conflict with `.gitignore` rules. Consider adding a brief contributor note about IDE sync to prevent future merge conflicts on this file.
- **Project Strategy (General)**: JDK 23 is a Short-Term Support (STS) release (EOL ~March 2025). If this project targets production stability, document the rationale for choosing an STS release over JDK 21 (LTS) or outline a migration path to JDK 25 (next LTS). Otherwise, the technical upgrade is well-structured.

Separating the GraalVM setup for CLI and GUI modes is a logical improvement, and updating the GluonFX plugin is necessary for Java 23 compatibility.   - 💡 **Architecture mapping clarity**: The expression `${{ matrix.os == 'macos-14' && 'aarch64' || 'x86_64' }}` works but implicitly assumes all non-`macos-14` runners are `x86_64`. Consider explicitly mapping `ubuntu-latest` and `windows-latest` to `amd64`/`x86_64` for readability, or extract architecture to a matrix variable.

- **`pom.xml`**:

Early Access builds are not recommended for production or stable releases. Upgrade to the stable `23.0.1` (or latest `23.x`) release to avoid potential runtime regressions or API changes.
  - 🔍 **Verify parent compiler settings**: Ensure the `<source>` and `<target>` properties in the parent `pom.xml` are explicitly updated to `23` to align with the module-level changes and prevent accidental fallbacks during clean builds.

The refactoring is applied consistently across metadata retrieval, split sourcing, and utility methods, improving performance for tables with many files or complex partition specs.

- `plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergSplitSource.java`: Replacing `wholeFileTask.spec()` and `fileScanTask.spec()` with `specsById` lookups is consistent. Storing the lookup result in a local variable (`spec` / `partitionSpec`) avoids redundant map accesses within the same method scope.
- `plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergUtil.java` & `StructLikeWrapperWithFieldIdToIndex.java`: The new `getFileScanPartitionSpec` and updated `getPartitionKeys`/`createStructLikeWrapper` signatures correctly shift spec resolution to the caller. Future iterations could consider adding `jdk-system-out` (to catch `System.out/err` usage) or `signatures-java11`/`signatures-java17` to enforce modern JDK practices. SUMMARY:
The PR introduces the forbiddenapis Gradle plugin (v3.8) with the `jdk-unsafe` bundled signature to detect usage of unsafe JDK APIs (e.g., Locale, Charset, Timezone classes). The configuration follows Kafka's existing pattern of chaining static analysis to the `test` task and uses `ignoreFailures = true` as a safe initial introduction.

POINTS:
- [build.gradle:798] The PR replaces the existing `test.dependsOn('checkstyleMain', 'checkstyleTest')` line with one that adds `forbiddenApisMain`. This is correct and consistent with how `spotbugsMain` is already wired (line 806: `test.dependsOn('spotbugsMain')`). However, note that Kafka does NOT use Gradle's built-in `check` task for static analysis aggregation—tools are chained to `test` instead.

- [build.gradle:800-804] The `forbiddenApis` configuration uses the plugin's extension DSL. This is valid syntax for the `de.thetaphi.forbiddenapis` plugin. The `:core` module sets `java.srcDirs = []` for its main source set (line 1336 in full file), so `forbiddenApisMain` will effectively be a no-op for `:core`—the same behavior as spotbugs, which also runs on all subprojects without explicit exclusion for `:core`.

- [build.gradle:803] Only `forbiddenApisMain` is added to the dependency chain; `forbiddenApisTest` is not included. This mirrors spotbugs' pattern (only `spotbugsMain` is wired; `spotbugsTest` is explicitly disabled project-wide via `gradle.taskGraph.whenReady` at lines 859-863). However, consider whether test code should also be scanned for forbidden APIs—unlike spotbugs, the forbiddenapis plugin does not have a corresponding suppression for test tasks.

- [build.gradle:803] `ignoreFailures = true` is set, which means violations will be reported but will not block the build. The PR description notes "ignoring failures for now." This is a reasonable approach for initial introduction, but a follow-up JIRA should be created to track removing this flag once violations are addressed.

- [build.gradle:801] Only `jdk-unsafe` is included in `bundledSignatures`. The comment references the full list of available signatures at the plugin wiki. Consider whether additional signatures like `jdk-system-out` (detects `System.out`/`System.err` usage) or `jdk-deprecated` would be valuable for Kafka's code quality goals.

- [build.gradle:801] The plugin version `3.8` is hardcoded in the `plugins` block. Kafka conventionally manages versions in `gradle/dependencies.gradle` (applied at line 25 via `apply from: "$rootDir/gradle/dependencies.gradle"`).
